volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Braggart Soldier (Section 2.6.560 - Section 3.1.690)

translation.label: Scaife English translation
translation.citation: Miles Gloriosus, or The Braggart Captain

sections.label: Section 2.6.560
sections.original: eam fieri apud me tam insignite iniuriam ?
sections.translation: such an injury so glaringly to be done to my neighbour ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.561
sections.original: Nunc demum a me insipienter factum esse arbitror ,
sections.translation: Now , at length , I am of opinion that it was done foolishly by me , when I come to understand the matter ; but still I did not do it with any ill intent .

sections.label: Section 2.6.563b
sections.original: Immo indigne ; nám hominem servom suos
sections.translation: Yes , but ’ twas improperly done ; for it befits a person that is a servant to keep his eyes , and hands , and talk , asleep .

sections.label: Section 2.6.565b
sections.original: Egone si post hunc diem
sections.translation: Now , if after this day I mutter anything , even what I know for certain , give me over to torture ; I ’ ll give myself up to you . This time , prithee , do pardon me for this .

sections.label: Section 2.6.568b
sections.original: Vincam animum meum ,
sections.translation: I shall subdue my feelings , so as to think that it was not done by you with malicious intent .

sections.label: Section 2.6.570
sections.original: ignoscam tibi istuc .
sections.translation: I will pardon you in this matter .

sections.label: Section 2.6.570b
sections.original: At tibi di faciant bene .
sections.translation: May the Gods bless you , then !

sections.label: Section 2.6.571
sections.original: Ne tu hercle , si te di ament , linguam comprimes
sections.translation: Troth now , as the Gods may prosper you , really do restrain your tongue henceforth ; even that which you do know , don ’ t know , and don ’ t you see what you do see .

sections.label: Section 2.6.573b
sections.original: Bene me mones ,
sections.translation: You counsel me aright ; so I ’ m resolved to do . Are you quite appeased ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.574b
sections.original: Abi .
sections.translation: Away with you .

sections.label: Section 2.6.575
sections.original: Numquid nunc aliud me vis ?
sections.translation: Is there aught else you now require of me ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.575b
sections.original: Ne me noveris .
sections.translation: That you would know me not .

sections.label: Section 2.6.576
sections.original: Dedit híc mihi verba . quam benigne gratiam
sections.translation: He has been cajoling me . How kindly he vouchsafed his favour not to be angry . I know what plan he is upon : that directly the Captain returns home from the Forum , I may be caught at home . He and Palaestrio together

sections.label: Section 2.6.580
sections.original: me habent venalem : sensi et iam dudum scio .
sections.translation: have me in their power : I have perceived that , and for some time I ’ ve known it . I ’ faith , never will I be seeking a bait this day from out of that wicker-net [*] . For now somewhither will I betake myself , and for some days will I lie concealed until this turmoil is hushed and their resentment is softened . Enough punishment for my unlucky prating have I already merited .

sections.label: Section 2.6.585
sections.original: verum tamen dé me quidquid est , ibo hinc domum .
sections.translation: But still , whatever befals me I ’ ll be off hence home .

sections.label: Section 2.6.586
sections.original: Illic hínc abscessit . sat edepol certo scio ,
sections.translation: So he has departed hence . I ’ faith , I know right well , that a dead pig full oft has more relish [*] by far than a living one : so bamboozled has he been , that he did not see what he really did see . For his eyes , and ears , and thoughts

sections.label: Section 2.6.590
sections.original: transfugere ad nos . usque adhuc actum est probe ;
sections.translation: have come over to us . So far , ’ tis right cleverly managed ; the lady has played her part most excellently . I ’ ll go back again to my Senate [*] ; for Palaestrio is now at home in my house , and now Sceledrus is gone from the door . A full Senate can now be held .

sections.label: Section 2.6.595
sections.original: ibo intro , ne , dum absum , alter sorti defuat . —
sections.translation: I ’ ll go in ; lest while I am absent , there should be a distribution [*] of their parts among them .

sections.label: Section 3.1.596
sections.original: Cóhibete intra limen etiam vos parumper , Pleusicles ,
sections.translation: Keep yourselves within [*] doors , yet a moment , Pleusicles . Let me first look out , that there may be no ambush anywhere , against that council which we intend to hold . For now we have need of a safe place from which no enemy can win the spoils of our counsels .

sections.label: Section 3.1.600
sections.original: nam bene consultum inconsultum est , si id inimicis usuist ,
sections.translation: For a well-devised plan is very often filched away , if the place for deliberating has not been chosen with care or with caution ; and what is well-advised is ill-advised if it proves of use to the enemy ; and if it proves of use to the enemy , it cannot otherwise than prove a detriment to yourself . For if the enemy learn your plans ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.605
sections.original: tuopte tibi consilio occludunt linguam et constringunt manus ,
sections.translation: by your own self-same plans they tie your tongue and bind your hands ; and they do the very same to you that you intended to do to them . But I ’ ll spy about , lest any one , either in this direction on the left or on the right , should come like a huntsman on our counsels with his ears like toils [*] . ( Looks about .) Quite vacant is the prospect hence right to the bottom of the street .

sections.label: Section 3.1.610
sections.original: evocabo . heus Periplectomene et Pleusicles , progredimini .
sections.translation: I ’ ll call them out . Hallo ! Periplecomenus and Pleusicles , come out !

sections.label: Section 3.1.611
sections.original: Ecce nos tibi oboedientes .
sections.translation: Behold us here obedient to your call .

sections.label: Section 3.1.611b
sections.original: Facilest imperium in bonis .
sections.translation: The sway is easy over the good . But I wish to know , if we are to carry out the matter on the same plan that we formed within ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.613b
sections.original: Magis nón potest esse ad rem utibile .
sections.translation: Why , in fact there ’ s nothing can be more conducive to our purpose .

sections.label: Section 3.1.613c
sections.original: Immo ⋯
sections.translation: Well , what say you , Pleusicles ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.614b
sections.original: Quodne vóbis placeat , displiceat mihi ?
sections.translation: Can that displease me which pleases yourselves ? What person is there more my friend than your own self ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.615b
sections.original: Loquere lepide et commode .
sections.translation: You speak kindly and obligingly .

sections.label: Section 3.1.616
sections.original: Pol ita decet hunc facere .
sections.translation: Faith , and so he ought to do .

sections.label: Section 3.1.616b
sections.original: At hoc me facinus miserum macerat
sections.translation: But this affair shockingly distresses me , and torments my very heart and body .

sections.label: Section 3.1.617b
sections.original: Quid id est quod cruciat ? cedo .
sections.translation: What is it that torments you ? Tell me .

sections.label: Section 3.1.618
sections.original: Me tibi istúc aetatis homini facinora puerilia
sections.translation: That I should cause childish actions in a person of your years , and that I should require of you deeds that neither become yourself nor your virtues ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.620
sections.original: ea te expetere ex opibus summis mei honoris gratia
sections.translation: and that , with all your might , for my sake you are striving to aid me in my passion , and are doing actions of such a kind , as , when done , these years of yours are wont rather to avoid than follow . I am ashamed that I cause you this trouble in your old age .

sections.label: Section 3.1.624
sections.original: Novo modo tu homo amás , siquidem te quicquam quod faxis pudet ;
sections.translation: You are a person in love after a new fashion . If , in fact , you are ashamed of anything you do ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.625
sections.original: nihil amas , umbra es amantis magis quam amator , Pleusicles .
sections.translation: you are nothing of a lover . You are rather the shadow of those who are in love , than a true lover , Pleusicles .

sections.label: Section 3.1.626
sections.original: Hancine aetatem exercere mei me amoris gratia ?
sections.translation: Ought I to employ these years of yours in seconding my love ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.627
sections.original: Quid ais tu ? itane tibi ego videor oppido Acherunticus ?
sections.translation: How say you ? Do I seem to you so very much a subject for Acheron [*] ? So much a bier ’ s-man [*] ? Do I seem to you to have had so very long a life ? Why , really , I am not more than four-and-fifty years old ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.630
sections.original: clare oculis video , pernix sum manibus , pedibus mobilis .
sections.translation: I see clearly with my eyes , I ’ m ready with my hands , I ’ m active with my feet .

sections.label: Section 3.1.631
sections.original: Si albicapillus hic , videtur neutiquam ab ingenio senex .
sections.translation: If he is seen by you to have white hair , he is by no means an old man in mind ; in him the natural strength of his mind is unimpaired .

sections.label: Section 3.1.633
sections.original: Pol id quidem éxperior ita esse ut praedicas , Palaestrio ;
sections.translation: By my troth , for my part , I have found it to be so as you say , Palaestrio ; for , in fact , his kindness is quite that of a young man .

sections.label: Section 3.1.635
sections.original: Immo , hospes , magis cúm periclum facies , magis nosces meam
sections.translation: Yes , my guest , the more you make trial of it , the more you will know my courtesy towards you in your love .

sections.label: Section 3.1.636b
sections.original: Quid opus nota noscere ?
sections.translation: What need to know what ’ s known already ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.637
sections.original: Vt apud te exemplum experiundi hábeas , ne quaeras foris ;
sections.translation: I ’ ll show you more amiability on my part than I ’ ll make mention of ⋯ that you may have instances for proving it at home , and not have to seek it out of doors . For unless one has loved himself , with difficulty he sees into the feelings of one in love .

sections.label: Section 3.1.639_640
sections.original: et ego amoris aliquantum habeo umorisque etiam in corpore ,
sections.translation: But I have some little love and moisture in my body still , and not yet am I dried up for the pursuits of merriment and pleasure . Either the merry banterer likewise , or the agreable boon-companion will I be ; no interrupter of another am I at a feast . I bear in mind how properly to keep myself from proving disagreable to my fellow-guests ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.645
sections.original: commemini et meae orationis iustam partem persequi
sections.translation: and how to take a due share with my conversation , and to be silent as well in my turn , when the discourse belongs to another . Far from being a spitter or hawker am I , far from being a dirty-nosed old fellow , too . And never do I take liberties with any person ’ s mistress when out in company ; I don ’ t snatch up the dainty bits before another , nor take the cup before my turn ; nor , through wine , do dissensions ever arise on my account at the convivial board . If there is any one there that is disagreable , I go off home ; I cut the parley short . Stretched at my ease , I devote myself to pleasure , love , and mirth . In fine , at Ephesus was I born , not among the Apulians , not at Animula [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.1.649
sections.original: O lepidum senem , in se si quas memorat virtutis habet ,
sections.translation: O what a most delightful old man , if he possesses the qualities he mentions !

sections.label: Section 3.1.650
sections.original: atque equidem plane educatum in nutricatu Venerio .
sections.translation: Why , troth , surely now , he was brought up in the very rearing of Venus .

sections.label: Section 3.1.657
sections.original: Tu quidem edepol omnis moris ad venustatem † vacet ;
sections.translation: By my troth , your whole manners really do show marks of first-rate breeding . Find me three men of such manners against a like weight in double-distilled gold [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.1.659
sections.original: At quidem illúc aetatis qui sit non invenies alterum
sections.translation: Why , in fact , you will not find another person who is of his years , more accomplished in every respect , or who is more a friend to his friend .

sections.label: Section 3.1.661
sections.original: Tute me ut fateare faciam esse adulescentem moribus ,
sections.translation: I ’ ll make you confess that I really am a youngster in my manners ; so abounding in kindnesses will I prove myself to you in every respect . Should you have need of an advocate , severe or fierce ? I am he . Have you need of one that is gentle ? You shall say that I am more gentle than the sea is when hush ’ d ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.665
sections.original: liquidiusculusque ero quam ventus est favonius .
sections.translation: and something more balmy will I prove than is the Zephyr breeze [*] . In this same person will I display to you either the most jovial boon-companion , or the first-rate trencher-man [*] , and the best of caterers . Then , as for dancing , there is no ballet-master that is so supple as I .

sections.label: Section 3.1.669
sections.original: Quid ad illas artis optassis , si optio eveniat tibi ?
sections.translation: What could you wish added to these accomplishments , if the option were given you ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.670
sections.original: Huius pro méritis ut referri pariter possit gratia ,
sections.translation: That thanks could be returned by me to him in degree equal to his deserts , and to yourself , to both of whom I feel that I am now the cause of extreme anxiety . But it is grievous to me to be the cause of so great expense to you .

sections.label: Section 3.1.672b
sections.original: Morus es .
sections.translation: You are a simpleton . For , if you lay anything out on a bad wife and upon an enemy , that is an expense ; that which is laid out on a deserving guest and a friend is gain ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.675
sections.original: et quod in divinis rebus sumptumst , sapienti lucrumst .
sections.translation: as that , which is expended upon sacred rites , is a profit to the wise man . By the blessing of the Gods , I have enough , with which to receive you with hospitality in my house . Eat , drink , indulge your tastes with me , and surfeit yourself with enjoyments ; my house is at your service , myself likewise do I wish to be at your service . For , through the blessing of the Gods , I may say that , by reason of my wealth ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.680
sections.original: licuit uxorem dotatam genere summo ducere ;
sections.translation: I could have married a dowered wife of the best family ; but I don ’ t choose to introduce an everlasting female barker at me into my house .

sections.label: Section 3.1.682
sections.original: Cur non vis ? nam procreare liberos lepidumst onus .
sections.translation: Why don ’ t you choose ? For ’ tis a delightful thing to be the father of children [ liberos ].

sections.label: Section 3.1.683
sections.original: Hercle vero liberum esse tete , id multo lepidiust .
sections.translation: Troth , ’ tis very much sweeter by far to be free [*] [ liberum ] yourself .

sections.label: Section 3.1.685
sections.original: Nam bona uxor suave ductust , si sit usquam gentium
sections.translation: For a good wife , if it is possible for her to be married anywhere on earth , where can I find her ? But am I to take one home who is never to say this to me , Buy me some wool , my dear , with which a soft and warm cloak may be made , and good winter under-clothes [*] , that you mayn ’ t catch cold this winter-weather ; such an expression as this you can never hear from a wife ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.690
sections.original: verum prius quam galli cantent quae me e somno suscitet ,
sections.translation: but , before the cocks crow , she awakes me from my sleep , and says , Give me some money , my dear , with which to make my mother a present on the Calends [*] , give me some money to make preserves ; give me something to give on the Quinquatrus [*] to the sorceress [*] , to the woman who interprets the dreams , to the prophetess , and to the female diviner ;
